audit_status.review_note: Compact complete draft reviewed for Judah's surety, Benjamin's descent, double silver, Joseph's house, brotherly seating, and Benjamin's favored portion.

verses.restored: The famine was heavy in the land.
When they had finished eating the grain they had brought from Egypt, their father said, Return, buy us a little food.
Judah spoke to him, saying, The man solemnly warned us, saying, You shall not see my face unless your brother is with you.
If you send our brother with us, we will go down and buy food for you.
But if you do not send him, we will not go down, for the man said to us, You shall not see my face unless your brother is with you.
Israel said, Why did you do harm to me by telling the man you had another brother?

They said, The man asked closely about us and our kindred, saying, Is your father still alive? Do you have a brother? We told him according to these words. Could we know that he would say, Bring your brother down?
Judah said to Israel his father, Send the boy with me, and we will rise and go, so that we may live and not die, both we, you, and our little ones.
I myself will be surety for him; from my hand you may require him. If I do not bring him back to you and set him before your face, I will bear the blame before you all the days.
If we had not delayed, surely by now we could have returned twice.

Israel their father said to them, If it must be so, do this: take from the choice produce of the land in your vessels and bring a gift down to the man: a little balm, a little honey, spices, myrrh, pistachios, and almonds.
Take double silver in your hand, and the silver returned in the mouth of your sacks you shall return in your hand; perhaps it was a mistake.
Take your brother also, rise, and return to the man.
May El Shaddai give you compassion before the man, so that he may release your other brother and Benjamin. As for me, if I am bereaved, I am bereaved.
The men took this gift, double silver in their hand, and Benjamin; they rose, went down to Egypt, and stood before Joseph.
Joseph saw Benjamin with them and said to the one over his house, Bring the men into the house, slaughter an animal, and prepare, for the men shall eat with me at noon.
The man did as Joseph said and brought the men into Joseph’s house.

The men were afraid because they were brought into Joseph’s house. They said, Because of the silver returned in our sacks at first we are being brought in, to roll himself upon us, fall upon us, and take us as servants with our donkeys.
They came near to the man over Joseph’s house and spoke to him at the entrance of the house.
They said, Please, my lord, we indeed came down at first to buy food.
When we came to the lodging place and opened our sacks, look, each man’s silver was in the mouth of his sack, our silver in full weight, and we have brought it back in our hand.
Other silver we have brought down in our hand to buy food. We do not know who put our silver in our sacks.
He said, Peace to you; do not fear. Your Divine and the Divine of your father gave you treasure in your sacks; your silver came to me. Then he brought Simeon out to them.
The man brought the men into Joseph’s house, gave water, and they washed their feet; he gave feed to their donkeys.
They prepared the gift for Joseph’s coming at noon, for they heard they would eat bread there.
When Joseph came home, they brought him the gift in their hand into the house and bowed down to him to the ground.
He asked them about peace and said, Is there peace with your aged father of whom you spoke? Is he still alive?
They said, There is peace with your servant our father; he is still alive. They bowed and prostrated themselves.
He lifted his eyes and saw Benjamin his brother, his mother’s son, and said, Is this your youngest brother of whom you spoke to me? He said, May the Divine be gracious to you, my son.
Joseph hurried, for his compassion grew warm toward his brother, and he sought a place to weep. He entered the chamber and wept there.
He washed his face, came out, restrained himself, and said, Set out bread.
They set out for him by himself, for them by themselves, and for the Egyptians eating with him by themselves, because Egyptians could not eat bread with Hebrews, for that is an abomination to Egypt.
They sat before him, the firstborn according to his firstborn status and the youngest according to his youth, and the men looked at one another in astonishment.
Portions were carried to them from before him, but Benjamin’s portion was five times greater than any of theirs. They drank and became merry with him.
